ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Salmon Fish Tikka; closest parent is the general salmon food product class. Marinated tandoor preparation is not represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW represents cooked salmon generically. No specific tikka or tandoor preparation code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226643001 identifies salmon as a substance. No specific tikka preparation concept exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Salmon Fish Tikka consists of approximately 3 to 4 chunks of marinated salmon, weighing around 200 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15237)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked Atlantic farmed salmon (dry heat), which closely represents the tandoor-roasted salmon fillet component. A modest upward adjustment to sodium_mg (~+80 mg per 100 g) is applied to account for the spiced yoghurt marinade (salt, spices). Other macros are taken directly from the USDA cooked salmon entry. ICMR-NIN does not publish a specific salmon tikka entry; USDA cooked salmon is the highest-quality defensible source for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Salmon Fish Tikka. GI is estimated at 30 based on the analogous category of high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked fish dishes. Protein-dominant foods with minimal starch consistently yield GI values in the 25–35 range in the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The small carbohydrate contribution (~2.5 g per 100 g) comes from the yoghurt marinade and spices; glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 5.0) / 100 = 1.5, confirming negligible glycemic impact. GI category is low (≤55).

hypertension.guidance: Salmon Fish Tikka contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g due to salt in the marinade; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and avoid additional salt or high-sodium accompaniments such as chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 30 and only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Salmon Fish Tikka has a negligible glycemic load and is a suitable high-protein, low-GI option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium-rich accompaniments are minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon is rich in long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), with approximately 4.1 g polyunsaturated fat per 100 g; regular consumption of oily fish such as salmon is associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk, making this dish a heart-favourable protein choice when prepared without excessive oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon Fish Tikka is an Indian tandoor-roasted fish dish consisting of boneless salmon chunks marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture containing ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, garam masala, and lemon juice, then cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is a dish with a lightly charred, aromatic exterior and a moist, flaky interior with a rich, smoky flavour profile. Salmon Fish Tikka is a popular starter or main course in North Indian and Indian restaurant cuisine worldwide, including in Singapore where it is served at upscale Indian restaurants such as Kinara at Boat Quay. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 206 kcal, 20.4 g protein, and 12.4 g fat per 100 g. Salmon is one of the richest dietary sources of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin D, vitamin B12, and selenium, making this dish nutritionally dense. The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~30), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible (~1.5), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate (~380 mg per 100 g) due to the marinade, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified salmon and dairy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in salmon fish tikka?
Is salmon tikka good for diabetics?
What spices are used in salmon tikka?
Is salmon tikka high in protein?
Salmon tikka vs chicken tikka nutrition
Can I eat salmon tikka on a low carb diet?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Salmon Fish Tikka exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked Atlantic salmon (dry heat), which is the highest-quality approved source available for this preparation. Sodium is adjusted upward to account for the spiced marinade. GI is an analogous estimate based on the protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate profile of the dish using the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level measurement exists in an approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked Atlantic farmed salmon (dry heat, NDB 15237 / FDC ID 175168). Sodium_mg is adjusted to approximately 380 mg per 100 g to reflect the salt contribution of the spiced yoghurt marinade; the USDA base value for plain cooked salmon is approximately 299 mg per 100 g. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; salmon contains negligible industrially produced trans fats — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked fish entries in Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for salmon tikka was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 5.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.5. 4. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no specific salmon tikka preparation code exists in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 5. Halal status is listed as true on the assumption that the restaurant uses halal-certified salmon and dairy; this should be verified at the restaurant level. 6. Price data (SGD 28.90) is preserved from the field survey source record for reference but is not used as a nutritional evidence source. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all micronutrients are sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central cooked salmon entry and are not sentinel values (-1); all are reported by the source.
